CSCE 442 Syllabus

Section 550 (55429)

Scientific Programming

Instructor Details

PM

Catalog Description

Scientific Programming. (3-0). Credit 3. Introduction to numerical algorithms

fundamental to scientific and engineering applications of computers; elementary

discussion of error; algorithms, efficiency; polynomial approximations, quadrature

and systems of algebraic and differential equations. Prerequisites: CSCE 221 with a

grade of C or better; MATH 304 or MATH 308 or concurrent enrollment.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 221 with a grade of C or better; MATH 304 or

MATH 308 or concurrent enrollment.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Apply computational arithmetic in real-world scenarios. Understand floating-point

numbers, rounding errors, relative and absolute errors, cancellation, and numerical

instability.

Solve linear and nonlinear equation systems, using Gaussian elimination, LU, QR

decompositions. Understand pivoting, norms, condition numbers, and apply

iterative methods, the bisection method, Newton’s method, the secant method.

Create and apply optimization techniques, based on the Golden section search, the

steepest descent method, and the Newton method, to solving practical problems.

Perform data interpolation for approximation through the application of Lagrange

interpolation, Newton interpolation, Hermite interpolation, and piecewise

polynomial interpolation.

Apply numerical integration and differentiation, Richardson extrapolation, the

steepest descent method, and the Newton method, to solving practical problems.

Perform data interpolation for approximation through the application of Lagrange

interpolation, Newton interpolation, Hermite interpolation, and piecewise

polynomial interpolation.

Apply numerical integration and differentiation, Richardson extrapolation, the

composite rule, Newton-Cotes quadrature, and Gaussian quadrature.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

Scientific Computing An Introductory Survey

ISBN:  9781611975581

Authors:  Michael T. Heath

Publisher:  SIAM

Publication Date:  2018-11-14

Grading Policy

Standard Letter Grading Scale:  A >= 90 > B >= 80 > C >= 70 > D >= 60 > F

Graded components:

Exams - 50%: There will be two (1 midterm + 1 final) equally weighted exams

Homework - 40%: There will be eight equally weighted homework

Class Engagement -10%: There will be in-class quizzes given throughout the

semester. On class days without a quiz, attendance will count as full credit for

class engagement

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

No penalty for late work if the student informs the TA or instructor before the

deadline with acceptable reasons. Otherwise, late work will receive a penalty

of 20% reduction of points per day, for maximum 5 days.

Course Schedule

Below is the planned but tentative schedule of topics and major assignments/exams. Note

that there will be homework assignments and in-class quizzes throughout as well. All

assignments and exam details will be communicated via Canvas.

Week 1 (January 12 - 16): Module 1 - Introduction (Computational Problems,

Approximations)

Week 2 (January 19 - 23): Module 1 - Introduction (Computer Arithmetic), Module

2 - Linear Equations (Review Linear Algebra)

Week 3 (January 26 - 30): Module 2 - Linear Equations (Existence & Uniqueness,

Solving Linear Systems)

Week 4 (February 2 - 6): Module 3 - Least Squares (Matrix Norm & Condition

Number, Existence & Uniqueness, Normal Equation)

Week 5 (February 9 - 13): Module 3 - Least Squares (QR Decomposition)

2 - Linear Equations (Review Linear Algebra)

Week 3 (January 26 - 30): Module 2 - Linear Equations (Existence & Uniqueness,

Solving Linear Systems)

Week 4 (February 2 - 6): Module 3 - Least Squares (Matrix Norm & Condition

Number, Existence & Uniqueness, Normal Equation)

Week 5 (February 9 - 13): Module 3 - Least Squares (QR Decomposition)

Week 6 (February 16 - 20): Module 4 - Interpolation (Polynomial Methods,

Interpolation Error)

Week 7 (February 23 - 27): Module 4 - Interpolation (Piecewise Polynomial

Interpolation),  Module 5 - Nonlinear Equations (Bisection & Newton Methods)

Week 8 (March 2 - 6):  Module 4 - Nonlinear Equations (Bisection & Newton's

Methods),  Midterm Exam

Week of March 9 - 13: Spring Break - No classes

Week 9 (March 16 - 20): Module 5 - Nonlinear Equations (Newton's Convergence

& Secant Method, System of Nonlinear Equations)

Week 10 (March 23 - 27): Module 6 - Optimization (Golden Section Search,

Newton's Method)

Week 11 (March 30 - April 3): Module 6 - Optimization (Multidimensional

Optimization Methods), Module 7 - Numerical Integration (Quadrature Rules)

Week 12 (April 6 - 10): Module 7 - Numerical Integration (Newton-Cotes

Quadrature, Gaussian Quadrature)

Week 13 (April 13 - 17): Module 8 - Numerical Differentiation & Richardson

Extrapolation

Week 14 (April 20 - 24): Module 9 - Initial Value Problems for ODEs

Week 15 (April 27 - May 1):  Final Exam Week

Important dates:

Midterm Exam: Thursday March 5, 2026, in class

Final Exam: Thursday April 30, 2026, 3:00 pm - 5:00 pm

AI Statement

This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students

themselves. Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any

substantive portion of an assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a

company to write assignments and using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT.
